fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the interop bridge tool we've been talking about. The basic idea is that we want managed-side code to be able to talk to browser script functions and vice versa, and we want to ship a single packaged file developers can drop into their projects without hand-writing a bunch of repetitive glue code every time. Think of it like that publish pipeline we built for the config module last quarter — same kind of idea where the tool does the heavy lifting at build time.

A few things that came up in last week's sync: the JSON number handling needs to be consistent on both sides of the wire (someone on the consumer team complained about mismatched values showing up), and all the different ways you can call a script function (sync, async, with or without a return value) need to route through whatever runtime is configured. Also the published output needs to support optional source maps and type declaration files, and there should be a way to clean up old files before writing new ones. If something can't be resolved during type generation, we should surface a clear error rather than silently skipping it.

The script bindings inside the published file need to handle nested namespaces from assembly names, and the two different annotation types (host-callable vs script-supplied) should behave differently. Type mappings for the declarations file need to follow the same conventions we use elsewhere in the project.

One quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for JSON numbers, if json_number_output is set to numbers_as_strings, that same exact setting has to be used both directions, not just on the way out. So the output should show outbound_number_output=WriteAsString and inbound_number_output=WriteAsString, and there isn’t a separate knob for each side. It’s one shared behavior applied symmetrically to outbound and inbound JSON processing, including the runtime configuration piece where the same JsonNumberHandling option is applied to both outbound (serializer) and inbound (deserializer) pipelines, as specified in feature1_1_runtime_configuration.json.

Also, when the runtime says something just isn’t available yet, we still want the result to include the call form, the forwarded identifier, and which channel it tried to use, and then finish with error=runtime_call_not_implemented. The call forms we care about are sync_without_result, sync_with_result, async_with_result, and async_without_result. sync_without_result and sync_with_result both use runtime_call=sync, while async_with_result and async_without_result both use runtime_call=async.

On the declaration side, the type mapping should stay simple and consistent: numeric types (int, float, double, long, etc.) map to number; bool maps to boolean; char and string map to string; DateTime and DateTimeOffset map to Date; void stays void; Task<T> and ValueTask<T> map to Promise<T> with T mapped recursively; Task and ValueTask map to Promise<void>; all other object types map to any.

A couple formatting specifics too. For a single-segment assembly like foo.dll, the declaration should be export declare const foo: { and then each member on its own indented line ending in a comma, then };. For a multi-segment assembly like bar.nya.dll, the outer segment wraps the inner using inline braces, so it starts export declare const bar: { nya: { and then the members are indented, and it closes with }}; on the same line as the last inner content. And if the input source snippet has no marked partial function declarations at all, the output should be completely empty — literally an empty string with no whitespace, no newlines, nothing.

Last piece is publishing behavior. Publishing always writes the main script library file and always reports library_published=true. Source maps only get written when emit_map is explicitly true; otherwise map_published=false. Type declarations only get written when emit_types is explicitly true; otherwise types_published=false. If assemblies is empty, the warnings count should still be warnings=0. This is all part of the same PublishTask / publisher flow that inspects compiled assemblies, generates the browser script library with bindings, optionally emits source maps and type declaration files, and handles base-directory cleaning — as described in Feature 3 of the PRD.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: PublishTask.Execute(assemblies, emitMap, emitTypes, clean, preexistingBaseFile) => PublishResult

trigger_keywords: what happens to old files when clean is disabled
preserve base file when not cleaning
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When clean is false (or not specified as true) and a preexisting base file exists, the file must be preserved and preexisting_base_file_exists=true must appear in the output. When clean is true (the default), any preexisting base file is removed before publishing and preexisting_base_file_exists=false is reported. The default behavior when the clean flag is absent is to clean.

trigger_keywords: how assembly name becomes script objects
shared root object declared only once
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each dot-separated segment of an assembly name (with .dll stripped) becomes a nested JavaScript object declaration in the published library. For example foo.bar.nya.dll produces exports.foo = {}; then exports.foo.bar = {}; then exports.foo.bar.nya = {};. If two assemblies share a root segment (e.g. foo.dll and foo.bar.dll), that root object must only be declared once — it must not be redeclared.

trigger_keywords: host callable versus script supplied binding
what does JSFunction emit vs JSInvokable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: JSInvokable marks a host-callable method and generates a script binding that calls the runtime invocation entry point, e.g. exports.foo.bar.nya.Bar = () => exports.invoke('foo.bar.nya', 'Bar');. JSFunction marks a script-supplied slot and generates an undefined placeholder, e.g. exports.bar.nya.Fun = undefined; — it is a slot to be assigned by the consumer, not a call to the runtime.

trigger_keywords: Task or ValueTask uses async invoke
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a JSInvokable method returns Task, Task<T>, ValueTask, or ValueTask<T>, the generated script binding must use the asynchronous invocation entry point (exports.invokeAsync or equivalent) rather than the synchronous exports.invoke. In the type declaration file, Task<bool> maps to Promise<boolean> and ValueTask maps to Promise<void>.

trigger_keywords: which runtime definitions are included in types file
Interop and Boot interfaces in declarations
import statements excluded from type definitions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The emitted type declaration bundle must include the runtime interface definitions export interface Interop {} and export interface Boot {} (both must be present). It must NOT include raw import statements or any unrelated declaration files. Only the supported runtime declaration resources are inlined — anything else is excluded.

trigger_keywords: what to output when declaration import cannot be resolved
error when supported declaration import missing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If a required supported declaration import cannot be resolved during type declaration generation, stdout must output the normalized error line error=type_import_missing. This should not abort the publishing process — it is reported as a neutral informational error rather than a fatal failure.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the two different annotation types should behave differently
context_pointers.actual_meaning: JSInvokable attribute generates an exports.invoke() call binding; JSFunction attribute generates an undefined slot — as tested in feature3_2_library_bindings.json and feature3_3_type_definitions.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: surface a clear error rather than silently skipping it
context_pointers.actual_meaning: When a required declaration import cannot be resolved, stdout must output error=type_import_missing (feature3_3_type_definitions.json); when a runtime call is unavailable, stdout must output error=runtime_call_not_implemented (feature1_2_runtime_routing.json)

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but it's not something I have specific context on from the original discussions. I'd suggest looking at the existing patterns in the codebase or checking with the engineering lead — they'd have more detail on that particular behavior than I do.

feature1_1_runtime_configuration.json.description: Configuring number serialization applies the same option to outbound and inbound JSON processing.

feature1_2_runtime_routing.json.description: Synchronous and asynchronous global calls are forwarded through the configured runtime with the requested identifier and normalized errors.

cases.expected_output:
call=sync_without_result
runtime_identifier=demo.echo
runtime_call=sync
error=runtime_call_not_implemented
call=sync_with_result
runtime_identifier=demo.echo
runtime_call=sync
error=runtime_call_not_implemented
call=async_with_result
runtime_identifier=demo.echo
runtime_call=async
error=runtime_call_not_implemented
call=async_without_result
runtime_identifier=demo.echo
runtime_call=async
error=runtime_call_not_implemented

feature2_1_partial_function_generation.j.description: Marked partial declarations are completed by generated forwarding bodies that call the corresponding global script identifier and pass declared parameters in order.

feature3_1_publish_artifacts.json.description: Publishing writes the boot library, optionally writes source maps and type definitions, controls base-directory cleaning, and reports inspection warnings without aborting.

feature3_2_library_bindings.json.description: The emitted browser library declares nested assembly objects and exposes method bindings according to imported and exported script directions.

feature3_3_type_definitions.json.description: The emitted type declaration bundle includes only allowed runtime definitions, declares assembly-shaped APIs, maps host types to script types, and reports missing imports with a neutral error.

value=true
contains=export interface Boot {}
value=true
contains=import from
value=false
contains=other
value=false

contains=AsyBool: () => Promise<boolean>
value=true

value=true
